item.title: Instapaper is a great cross-platform and cloud-based read it later app for your bookmarks
item.itunes:title: Instapaper is a great cross-platform and cloud-based read it later app for your bookmarks
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
After switching to a new browser, I've been trying to better organize my links and bookmarks. While I settled on a useful start page that also includes my bookmarks, I also wanted to make it easier to save articles that I want to read later. My new browser does have a way to save pages to visit later, but I'm currently not syncing my desktop and mobile apps.

As a result, I decided to try a standalone read-it-later app that I can easily sync across devices. Instapaper stood out as a user-friendly option, and after trying it for the past week, it has proven to be an impressive and convenient tool.

What is Instapaper?

A cross-platform link-saving app

At its core, Instapaper is a read-it-later app that can be used on both the web and mobile devices. However, besides saving links to articles you want to catch up on later, you can also use it for bookmarking, saving videos you want to watch, saving notes and comments about certain articles, and organizing links using various tags. If you want to keep your home dashboard relatively uncluttered, you can also use folders to organize links into specific tabs.

While the platform is free, it does also have a premium subscription. With a paid plan, you can save links as PDFs, access full-text search, create a permanent archive, and add unlimited notes.

There are a variety of ways to add links and bookmarks to Instapaper. My favorite ways are to send links to the Android app using the share button, as well as using the Instapaper browser extension. You can also manually add links on the app and website if you don't mind copying and pasting.

Another way is to forward links to a personal address that Instapaper provides. This is useful for newsletters that have a variety of links you want to look at later. You can also set up various automations on IFTTT with Instapaper.

Getting started with Instapaper

You first need to create an account

To use Instapaper, you will need to create an account. This ensures that you can keep your links synced across devices and is also required for the browser extension to function properly. To get the most out of the platform, I recommend installing its mobile app on your phone and the browser extension on your main browser.

You can then get started with saving and organizing your links. I created a few tags to help me categorize links into different views: Self-hosting, Poetry, and NotebookLM. I also created a few folders to sort my links and keep my home dashboard clean. These folders included Research (for articles related to things I'm writing about), Bookmarks (for links to web pages I use as shortcuts or struggle to remember), and Read Later (for things I just want to catch up on later).

I fiddled around with the notes tool to see how it worked, but it ended up being the tool I used the least. To add a note to an article, you can highlight text and click on the plus icon that pops up. You can then revisit the note later by visiting the Notes tab.

I was also interested to find that when you open an article within Instapaper, you can generate a summary for it. This is convenient if you want to get a quick summary of the article's main points. I generally prefer to read articles for myself, but I know that for longer texts, this feature would really come in handy for finding the meat of the text.

Easily make bookmarks and links available across devices

Instapaper is user-friendly for beginners

Something I like about Instapaper is that it's incredibly easy to get started with. I've tried out other apps to save links, including self-hosted ones like Karakeep and Linkwarden. These are useful for preserving your privacy and maintaining control over your data, but some setup is required, and things become more complicated when you want to access these services outside your home network.

If you want a setup that doesn't require your own server, but is also available for free, Instapaper is a great option. Because of its organizational features, you can use it to do more than simply save articles to read later. I previously set up Raindrop.io to serve as a bookmark and read later hub, but I find I prefer it for bookmarks only. Having a separate space for articles and videos I want to view later actually works better for me.

I noticed that Instapaper requires minimal setup and can accurately generate previews of links without needing to be edited. I also like that it automatically sorts video links into the video folder.

Another benefit of Instapaper is that it stores its own version of the article. This means that it removes many annoying site elements, but also means that you can access your articles while you're offline. This makes it perfect for catching up on interesting articles while you're on public transit or a flight. This doesn't work for videos, however.

You should also note that while it stores an in-app page for posts on platforms like Reddit, you can't see comments without visiting the original link. This is also true for articles that have comment threads.

Another benefit of the app is that you can adjust text size, font, and background color. This is a useful accessibility feature, especially if your mobile browser doesn't support a reader mode. The mobile app also supports text-to-speech, another valuable accessibility feature.

Instapaper balances simplicity and functionality

There are plenty of options when it comes to read-it-later apps, but I've found that Instapaper includes the essential features I need without too much bloat. There are self-hosted alternatives to Pocket, but my NAS's limited RAM has meant that I've had to prioritize exactly which apps I want to self-host.

If you're looking for a convenient cross-platform solution with a minimal learning curve, I'd recommend trying Instapaper.

Instapaper
See at Instapaper

item.title: The only free graphic editors I use all share this one feature
item.itunes:title: The only free graphic editors I use all share this one feature
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Jump Links

  • What is non-destructive editing?
  • Which free editors are truly non-destructive?

I’ve tested a lot of free graphics editors over the years. From basic sketch tools to surprisingly polished Photoshop alternatives. Some look great on the surface but crumble once you start layering edits or pushing them past the basics. Eventually, I started noticing a pattern. The tools that actually stick with me all share one thing in common.

It’s not the interface, or how many filters and brush options they have. It’s something a bit deeper in how they handle your edits and layers. After spending enough time bouncing between editing apps, I realized it’s this one feature that changes how fluid and forgiving the creative process feels: non-destructive editing.

What is non-destructive editing?

And how is it different from destructive editing?

Destructive editing permanently alters the image data. Every adjustment, whether it’s a filter or simple slider change, rewrites the pixels themselves. Once applied, those changes become part of the layer. Reversing this usually requires undoing your steps (with an undo shortcut like Ctrl + Z) or starting over from scratch. With destructive editing, experimentation carries the consequences of irreversible changes, or at least the tedious reworking of your steps if you undo the changes.

Non-destructive editing keeps the pixels intact. It means you can make changes to an image, graphic, or illustration without changing the original data. So instead of baking your edits directly into the layer, the app keeps the adjustments separate through features like adjustment layers, smart objects, masks, groups, or editable version history. These features are the “carriers” of your edits, and they can be applied to the original layer to create the same effects you would get with destructive editing. But the difference is that you can tweak or remove them later on because they’re isolated from the original layer, so the original image remains intact.

Think of it like a notepad. Drawing directly on the notepad with a pen permanently changes the state of the paper, which is destructive. But if you draw each element of your drawing on a sticky note, you can build a full drawing by layering the little sticky notes on top of the notepad, and swap, alter, or remove them as you go. The paper underneath remains unaltered and is in the same state as when you started, meaning you drew non-destructively.

This is also why vector graphics are inherently non-destructive. Unlike raster graphics, they don’t use pixels and instead are defined by mathematical formulas that determine the colors and shapes. This allows for infinite scaling and unlimited alteration without degrading the original data.

Which free editors are truly non-destructive?

They’re few and far between

Most free graphics editors claim to be powerful, and some genuinely are, but very few actually let you work without locking in every change you make. Even GIMP, one of the most renowned free open-source graphics editors out there, isn’t a true non-destructive editor. You have to employ some tedious workarounds to edit “non-destructively” in apps like that. Here are the apps that offer true non-destructive editing, and how they do it:

Photopea

Photopea is the browser-based mirror of Photoshop, so you’ll find almost all the same features and functions in it, including non-destructive editing.

Photopea lets you edit non-destructively mainly by creating adjustment layers – it’s the little half-circle icon in the bottom-right, which opens a long menu of adjustment options like colors and HSL. You can also create a mask if you want the adjustments to apply only to a specific part of the image (via the square icon with a little circle in it). Both of these options create an additional layer, which you’ll see as an extra square shape on your selected layer. You can adjust them through the Properties settings in the top right (icon with three little lines).

Another way Photopea lets you edit non-destructively is through Smart Objects, just like in Photoshop. Converting your layer to a Smart Object lets you apply filters and transformations that you can adjust in a new tab, or remove if needed. It’s like a little container that holds all your adjustments.

Photopea
See at Photopea

Figma and Penpot

I’m putting Figma and Penpot together here as they’re practically mirrors of each other. These UX design apps handle non-destructive editing a bit differently: instead of adjustment layers or smart objects, everything in Figma and Penpot exists as live, independent, editable objects from the start.

When you apply a Boolean operation, a transformation, or an effect to your shapes or text, it doesn’t overwrite the original data – it creates a sort of container that holds the edits separately. Groups and components work the same way at a higher level, letting you move, scale, and adjust multiple objects without touching the original shapes inside. Ultimately, you can resize, color, and transform elements at any point, and none of it's permanent.

This architecture also fits their collaborative nature. In UI and UX design, it’s crucial to keep everything reversible and adjustable; one person’s adjustments shouldn’t permanently alter a shared design.

Figma
See at Figma
Penpot
See at Penpot

Another RawTherapee

ART (Another RawTherapee) is a fork of the free open-source RAW image editor, RawTherapee. This is a quirky editor in that it has a unique layout and feature set. Its color grading controls go above and beyond what you’d expect to find in a free raw editor, and it also has extras like batch editing. But what I like most about ART is how it handles non-destructive editing.

On the left, there’s a History panel containing all the adjustments you’ve made to your image. Clicking on any of the adjustments lets you adjust those features from exactly where you’ve left them – this is non-destructive! An editable version history is a bit different from how most graphics apps handle non-destructive editing, but I love how ART implements it.

Art
See at Pixls

This feature matters more than people realize

Non-destructive editing isn’t just a technical detail, but also a creative safety net. It gives you room to explore ideas without the pressure of getting it right the first time or losing all your progress. In some apps, it’s even a critical part of collaborative workflows. Once you get used to non-destructive editing, it’s hard to commit to apps that destructively and permanently bake every adjustment into your graphics.

Photopea lets you edit non-destructively mainly by creating adjustment layers – it’s the little half-circle icon in the bottom-right, which opens a long menu of adjustment options like colors and HSL. You can also create a mask if you want the adjustments to apply only to a specific part of the image (via the square icon with a little circle in it). Both of these options create an additional layer, which you’ll see as an extra square shape on your selected layer. You can adjust them through the Properties settings in the top right (icon with three little lines).

Another way Photopea lets you edit non-destructively is through Smart Objects, just like in Photoshop. Converting your layer to a Smart Object lets you apply filters and transformations that you can adjust in a new tab, or remove if needed. It’s like a little container that holds all your adjustments.

Photopea
See at Photopea

Figma and Penpot

I’m putting Figma and Penpot together here as they’re practically mirrors of each other. These UX design apps handle non-destructive editing a bit differently: instead of adjustment layers or smart objects, everything in Figma and Penpot exists as live, independent, editable objects from the start.

When you apply a Boolean operation, a transformation, or an effect to your shapes or text, it doesn’t overwrite the original data – it creates a sort of container that holds the edits separately. Groups and components work the same way at a higher level, letting you move, scale, and adjust multiple objects without touching the original shapes inside. Ultimately, you can resize, color, and transform elements at any point, and none of it's permanent.

This architecture also fits their collaborative nature. In UI and UX design, it’s crucial to keep everything reversible and adjustable; one person’s adjustments shouldn’t permanently alter a shared design.

Figma
See at Figma
Penpot
See at Penpot

Another RawTherapee

ART (Another RawTherapee) is a fork of the free open-source RAW image editor, RawTherapee. This is a quirky editor in that it has a unique layout and feature set. Its color grading controls go above and beyond what you’d expect to find in a free raw editor, and it also has extras like batch editing. But what I like most about ART is how it handles non-destructive editing.

On the left, there’s a History panel containing all the adjustments you’ve made to your image. Clicking on any of the adjustments lets you adjust those features from exactly where you’ve left them – this is non-destructive! An editable version history is a bit different from how most graphics apps handle non-destructive editing, but I love how ART implements it.

Art
See at Pixls

This feature matters more than people realize

Non-destructive editing isn’t just a technical detail, but also a creative safety net. It gives you room to explore ideas without the pressure of getting it right the first time or losing all your progress. In some apps, it’s even a critical part of collaborative workflows. Once you get used to non-destructive editing, it’s hard to commit to apps that destructively and permanently bake every adjustment into your graphics.

item.title: The 4 reasons why Windows 11 users are switching to Linux
item.itunes:title: The 4 reasons why Windows 11 users are switching to Linux
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Windows is still the world’s dominant desktop operating system, but that dominance is being tested. What was once a platform built around efficiency and choice now feels increasingly bloated, ad-filled, and inconsistent. You can’t open the Start menu without seeing a suggestion you didn’t ask for, or browse your files without feeling like you're being watched, or at worst, sold a service. If Microsoft wants to stop users from jumping ship to Linux, it’s not about reinventing Windows, it’s about fixing what people already hate about it.

Cut the bloat

Offer a clean install for those who want it

Windows 7 was the last Windows OS that was actually fresh when you installed it. There was very little bloat in the form of apps or features that users didn't want, and it really felt like a blank slate. With each passing Windows version, more useless bloat has been added. I do not want a preinstalled TikTok app. I do not want a preinstalled LinkedIn app. I definitely do not want Candy Crush preinstalled either, and I imagine the vast majority of Windows users share this sentiment. Even if it's a suggestion in my Start menu, that's too much for a lot of users, and it's pushing them towards cleaner operating systems.

Linux distros can be as minimal or as feature-packed as you like. It really depends on which distro you choose, but in all cases it's made really clear what is being installed on your computer at the start. Some distros are completely clean installs, some let the user decide what apps and packages they'd like at the start, and others come with many useful apps and even games.

All users deserve here is a choice. When installing Windows fresh, or setting up a device for the first time, offering a "clean install" option or some kind of "custom" option when undergoing setup would remove a lot of the headache surrounding bloatware on Windows.

Stop pushing ads

Literally nobody wants these in their operating system

The last thing anyone wants when they're trying to use their computer is more ads. They're unavoidable when you go online, but when you're just performing operations on your local machine, ads should not be present. Experimenting with ads in the Start menu is one thing, but putting them in File Explorer and even the Settings app is so egregious. Displaying Game Pass offers or MSN content on my lock screen is so unnecessary and makes my OS feel dirty and tainted.

Linux environments feature no ads. You're not being sold a subscription when browsing your files, and you don't feel like cattle when you're just trying to change settings. Microsoft needs to understand that just because everything can be an advertising channel, doesn't mean everything should be one. A simple toggle to remove any and all ads from the OS would be a sufficient gesture.

Improve privacy transparency

Give users the control they want

While you're able to opt out of most of the basic data collection when setting up Windows, a lot of the advanced stuff unfortunately stays on, and isn't exactly straightforward to completely rid your system of. Even when opting out, you're still delivering a lot of your data to Microsoft. To make matters even worse, Microsoft lost a ton of goodwill (and customers) when they began launching Recall, and lost another swath when it was exposed for having very serious security faults that had the potential to seriously compromise privacy.

While some Linux distros do collect telemetry data, all of it is very easy to opt out of. While I imagine the Recall debacle was a bridge too far for a lot of users, if Microsoft were to implement some sort of "Lockdown Mode" or "Privacy Mode" global toggle that disables all telemetry and online sync features, I think that'd do a lot to earn the trust of some users back.

Improve consistency of the UI

Again, give users the choice

While they have made some good changes with Windows 10 and 11, the UX is still so inconsistent, and while I have no issues with navigating it, that's because of my experience, and users who are less familiar are undoubtedly having trouble finding what they're looking for. The crux of this, for me, is the Settings app, and how it continues to have completely different functionality to the Control Panel. Add in modern right-click context menus, and you have a recipe to confuse a lot of new users, and frustrate a lot of old ones.

While Linux isn't exactly consistent across the board because of its different distros and very different GUI environments that are available, once you choose one, the design is unified. The settings and design language are consistent across them, and any changes you want to make have probably been thought of and implemented by someone else already, making customizations relatively easy.

For Windows, the solution is pretty simple: pick a lane. Either add the elements that are missing from the modern context and Settings menus, or just scrap it and rely on the Control Panel. The weird overlapping has gone on far too long.

It's all about rebuilding user trust

Windows doesn’t need to become Linux. Having a choice between them is great. Microsoft just needs to remember that its users value choice, clarity, and control. Give them the option to install cleanly, to turn off ads, to protect their privacy, and to navigate through a consistent interface. Do that, and users will stop looking for alternatives.

item.title: Motherboard features I care about a lot more than the chipset
item.itunes:title: Motherboard features I care about a lot more than the chipset
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
When you're buying a motherboard, the chipset is a shorthand for the kind of features and quality you can expect. A B650 or B850 chipset will entail a relatively reduced feature set compared to an X670 or X870 chipset. A higher-end chipset might give you access to more Gen5 lanes and universal PCIe 5.0 connectivity, but, for most people, the real differences between motherboards lie in features like high-quality VRMs, decked-out I/O, and onboard Wi-Fi, and these aren't tied to the chipset.

A Gen5 M.2 slot

Slowly becoming the standard

Gen5 SSDs might not be relevant for most users, but you should still have at least one PCIe 5.0 M.2 slot on your motherboard. If you're buying a motherboard in 2025, having Gen5 storage support is necessary even if you aren't planning to install a Gen5 SSD yet. The prices of blazing-fast Gen5 SSDs will keep dropping, and games and other applications will gradually leverage their read/write speeds. You need to be prepared for when that happens. Besides, even today's budget motherboards feature a single Gen5 M.2 slot — it has started to become a standard.

Gen5 storage support isn't mandatory on every chipset, hence it's better to focus on the spec sheet instead of the chipset mentioned in the motherboard's name. For those who're ready to move to Gen5 SSDs right away, multiple Gen5 M.2 slots should be at the top of the priority list. Fortunately, you don't need to spend a bomb for that privilege; a $250 motherboard will provide you with dual Gen5 and dual Gen4 M.2 slots.

Asus ROG Strix B850-F Gaming WiFi
$255 $300 Save $45
7.5/10
Memory Slots
4
Memory Type
DDR5-8000
Form Factor
ATX

Looking for a mid-range AMD AM5 motherboard for the latest processors? Look no further than the Asus ROG Strix B850-F Gaming WiFi. This thing has everything you'd need from a modern motherboard, including tasteful RGB lighting, excellent VRM cooling, plenty of connectivity options, and space for countless M.2 SSDs.

$255 at Amazon

Onboard Wi-Fi and Bluetooth

A must-have feature still absent from cheap boards

In 2025, onboard wireless connectivity might be the bare minimum, but some motherboards still don't come with onboard Wi-Fi and Bluetooth. If you're building a starter PC, and have a tight budget to play with, you might have to sacrifice onboard Wi-Fi when choosing a motherboard. Wi-Fi shouldn't be optional on motherboards anymore, and users shouldn't be forced to buy a Wi-Fi adapter, but that's how it is. Thankfully, once you cross the $130 mark, almost every motherboard will sport Wi-Fi 6 or Wi-Fi 6E connectivity alongside Bluetooth.

You'll realize the importance of onboard Wi-Fi when your PC is out of reach of a wired connection. Whether a temporary or permanent situation, having the feature as a backup is always recommended.

ASRock B650M PG Lightning WiFi
$120 $170 Save $50

The ASRock B650M PG Lightning WiFi is an incredible value-for-money motherboard for DDR5 systems, providing 3 M.2 ports (including a Gen5 port), WiFi 6E, 2.5G LAN, and BIOS flashback. Its mATX form factor doesn't sacrifice features or performance. 
 

$120 at Amazon

QoL features

Making life easy for all

QoL or quality-of-life features on motherboards are underappreciated additions that enhance the PC building experience. Whether it's the M.2 slots with pre-installed heatsinks or tool-less functionality, BIOS flashback, or a 7-segment display for debugging, these little additions not only make a motherboard feel more premium, but also improve your experience each time you remove your graphics card or update your BIOS. Many motherboards now come with quick-release PCIe slots and dual BIOS to simplify regular PC maintenance.

Even onboard power and reset buttons that most people can do without are welcome additions on any motherboard. These features might not be directly tied to performance, but they play a significant role in the overall PC building experience.

Ample fan and RGB headers

You know you'll need them sooner or later

There's nothing worse than running out of motherboard headers when installing case fans or ARGB cables. While most motherboards have a decent number of fan and RGB headers, you need to inspect this closely to determine whether it'll be enough for the components you're planning to connect to the board. Not every motherboard will satisfy every user — someone might be content with the stock fans, while someone else might need all the headers they can get.

Being forced to buy a fan or RGB hub even after buying an expensive motherboard sucks big time. Before you part with your money, check the detailed spec sheet of the motherboard to ensure you're getting enough headers — at least 4 system fan headers, 3 ARGB headers, and 1 RGB header is what I recommend.

Quality VRMs

A non-negotiable

You buy a motherboard not just for its features and compatibility, but also for durability. An entry-level motherboard might be fine for a home office PC, but gamers and professionals who intend to stress their hardware a bit more need to consider the VRM quality seriously. The VRM design of your motherboard determines how well it can supply clean and reliable power to your CPU, memory, and other components. If you're planning to run mid-range or high-end processors and RAM, you should value quality VRMs that can tackle your hardware without thermal throttling.

Motherboard reviews and roundups often list VRM temperatures, and will tell you which motherboard model is worth investing in. Often, more expensive motherboards fare worse in these tests, making it clear that the chipset alone doesn't guarantee a high-quality VRM design.

A robust I/O stack

It's more important than you think

Finally, a capable I/O stack is a must for almost all gamers, enthusiasts, and professionals. The number of USB devices on a modern PC is nothing to be scoffed at — keyboards, mice, headphones, speakers, external drives, VR headsets, and more. Even wireless peripherals need dedicated USB-A or USB-C ports for their dongles. The USB speeds need to be sufficiently high to accommodate the demands of modern devices. This makes features like 20Gbps USB ports, USB-C support, and multi-gig Ethernet more important than ever before.

You might not need all the I/O functionality on day one, but you might need it when you buy more peripherals and connect other wired or wireless devices over time. The I/O stack on most budget motherboards feels lacking by modern standards, so you might have to upgrade to a mid-range model if you need sufficient USB ports and high-speed Ethernet.

The motherboard chipset matters, but only so much

When choosing a motherboard for your CPU, you obviously need to pick the right chipset. However, the rest of the functionality and quality of your motherboard isn't guaranteed by a high-end chipset. It depends on the price of the motherboard and the choices made by the manufacturer on a particular model. Features like high-quality VRMs, onboard Wi-Fi, and an extensive I/O stack are more important than many people realize. So, the next time you're shopping for a motherboard, focus on everything instead of just the chipset.

Gen5 SSDs might not be relevant for most users, but you should still have at least one PCIe 5.0 M.2 slot on your motherboard. If you're buying a motherboard in 2025, having Gen5 storage support is necessary even if you aren't planning to install a Gen5 SSD yet. The prices of blazing-fast Gen5 SSDs will keep dropping, and games and other applications will gradually leverage their read/write speeds. You need to be prepared for when that happens. Besides, even today's budget motherboards feature a single Gen5 M.2 slot — it has started to become a standard.

Gen5 storage support isn't mandatory on every chipset, hence it's better to focus on the spec sheet instead of the chipset mentioned in the motherboard's name. For those who're ready to move to Gen5 SSDs right away, multiple Gen5 M.2 slots should be at the top of the priority list. Fortunately, you don't need to spend a bomb for that privilege; a $250 motherboard will provide you with dual Gen5 and dual Gen4 M.2 slots.

Asus ROG Strix B850-F Gaming WiFi
$255 $300 Save $45
7.5/10
Memory Slots
4
Memory Type
DDR5-8000
Form Factor
ATX

Looking for a mid-range AMD AM5 motherboard for the latest processors? Look no further than the Asus ROG Strix B850-F Gaming WiFi. This thing has everything you'd need from a modern motherboard, including tasteful RGB lighting, excellent VRM cooling, plenty of connectivity options, and space for countless M.2 SSDs.

$255 at Amazon

Onboard Wi-Fi and Bluetooth

A must-have feature still absent from cheap boards

In 2025, onboard wireless connectivity might be the bare minimum, but some motherboards still don't come with onboard Wi-Fi and Bluetooth. If you're building a starter PC, and have a tight budget to play with, you might have to sacrifice onboard Wi-Fi when choosing a motherboard. Wi-Fi shouldn't be optional on motherboards anymore, and users shouldn't be forced to buy a Wi-Fi adapter, but that's how it is. Thankfully, once you cross the $130 mark, almost every motherboard will sport Wi-Fi 6 or Wi-Fi 6E connectivity alongside Bluetooth.

You'll realize the importance of onboard Wi-Fi when your PC is out of reach of a wired connection. Whether a temporary or permanent situation, having the feature as a backup is always recommended.

ASRock B650M PG Lightning WiFi
$120 $170 Save $50

The ASRock B650M PG Lightning WiFi is an incredible value-for-money motherboard for DDR5 systems, providing 3 M.2 ports (including a Gen5 port), WiFi 6E, 2.5G LAN, and BIOS flashback. Its mATX form factor doesn't sacrifice features or performance. 
 

$120 at Amazon

QoL features

Making life easy for all

QoL or quality-of-life features on motherboards are underappreciated additions that enhance the PC building experience. Whether it's the M.2 slots with pre-installed heatsinks or tool-less functionality, BIOS flashback, or a 7-segment display for debugging, these little additions not only make a motherboard feel more premium, but also improve your experience each time you remove your graphics card or update your BIOS. Many motherboards now come with quick-release PCIe slots and dual BIOS to simplify regular PC maintenance.

Even onboard power and reset buttons that most people can do without are welcome additions on any motherboard. These features might not be directly tied to performance, but they play a significant role in the overall PC building experience.

Ample fan and RGB headers

You know you'll need them sooner or later

There's nothing worse than running out of motherboard headers when installing case fans or ARGB cables. While most motherboards have a decent number of fan and RGB headers, you need to inspect this closely to determine whether it'll be enough for the components you're planning to connect to the board. Not every motherboard will satisfy every user — someone might be content with the stock fans, while someone else might need all the headers they can get.

Being forced to buy a fan or RGB hub even after buying an expensive motherboard sucks big time. Before you part with your money, check the detailed spec sheet of the motherboard to ensure you're getting enough headers — at least 4 system fan headers, 3 ARGB headers, and 1 RGB header is what I recommend.

Quality VRMs

A non-negotiable

You buy a motherboard not just for its features and compatibility, but also for durability. An entry-level motherboard might be fine for a home office PC, but gamers and professionals who intend to stress their hardware a bit more need to consider the VRM quality seriously. The VRM design of your motherboard determines how well it can supply clean and reliable power to your CPU, memory, and other components. If you're planning to run mid-range or high-end processors and RAM, you should value quality VRMs that can tackle your hardware without thermal throttling.

Motherboard reviews and roundups often list VRM temperatures, and will tell you which motherboard model is worth investing in. Often, more expensive motherboards fare worse in these tests, making it clear that the chipset alone doesn't guarantee a high-quality VRM design.

A robust I/O stack

It's more important than you think

Finally, a capable I/O stack is a must for almost all gamers, enthusiasts, and professionals. The number of USB devices on a modern PC is nothing to be scoffed at — keyboards, mice, headphones, speakers, external drives, VR headsets, and more. Even wireless peripherals need dedicated USB-A or USB-C ports for their dongles. The USB speeds need to be sufficiently high to accommodate the demands of modern devices. This makes features like 20Gbps USB ports, USB-C support, and multi-gig Ethernet more important than ever before.

You might not need all the I/O functionality on day one, but you might need it when you buy more peripherals and connect other wired or wireless devices over time. The I/O stack on most budget motherboards feels lacking by modern standards, so you might have to upgrade to a mid-range model if you need sufficient USB ports and high-speed Ethernet.

The motherboard chipset matters, but only so much

When choosing a motherboard for your CPU, you obviously need to pick the right chipset. However, the rest of the functionality and quality of your motherboard isn't guaranteed by a high-end chipset. It depends on the price of the motherboard and the choices made by the manufacturer on a particular model. Features like high-quality VRMs, onboard Wi-Fi, and an extensive I/O stack are more important than many people realize. So, the next time you're shopping for a motherboard, focus on everything instead of just the chipset.

item.title: 4 reasons why you might want to roll back to a previous Windows update
item.itunes:title: 4 reasons why you might want to roll back to a previous Windows update
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
​Windows Updates have done more for my PCs over the years than anything else. They’ve arrived in waves of helpful, important, and often necessary changes and OS features for decades. But sometimes they also make me want to donate my computer to the nearest hungry, hungry hydraulic press to be crushed into a billion pieces.

Although helpful, Windows Updates have caused their fair share of problems. An update can ship in a faulty state, a program might not play well with it, or a long list of other possible, entirely too probable scenarios may occur. What’s more, the horrid results range from the menial to the downright catastrophic. Usually, I can wait for Microsoft to push a fix — no harm, no foul — but manually rolling back the update is sometimes my best choice, and here's why it might be good for you too.

Windows ships a faulty update

Bricked drives, erased files, and BSODs, oh my!

A faulty Windows update is a straightforward instance of deciding whether to roll back, because, frankly, they can do the most damage, and it might even be your only option to deal with it.

Most recently, for example, was Microsoft’s now-infamous August 2025 update (version KB5063878), which left quite the aftermath in its wake. This update caused several serious issues with Phison-branded drives, including data loss, the drive disappearing from the OS completely, and, in some cases, permanent drive corruption. Phison and Windows each denied any wrongdoing (classic), but the damage was nonetheless done. Eventually, the Chinese PC enthusiast Facebook group PCDIY! investigated and found the issue stemmed from a piece of pre-release firmware within certain Phison drives. This case was particularly egregious, as there isn't much worse an update can do than brick a drive.

Even the less devastating cases can still be deeply inconvenient, though. As was the case in 2018 for the 1809 version of Windows 10, which fully deleted huge chunks of user files of all types. In cases reaching this scale, the media pounces to cover it. Tech and PC news sites, YouTube channels, and even Reddit users flooded the news cycle with discussions about the findings.

That fact alone has completely changed how I have dealt with faulty Windows updates for the past several years. The issue’s severity, lending itself to a dramatic news cycle, became quite the silver lining. With so many people affected, I could always find coverage and key information with a quick online search. This makes it much easier to decide that it’s time to roll back the update.

Bugs, errors, and other smaller problems

A smaller scale can still mean big frustration

It’s certainly possible that you’ll encounter something so severe with your Windows Updates that it makes headlines. It’s much more likely, however, that you’ll run into something on a smaller scale.

One such experience occurred for me shortly after the Windows 11 update was launched in late 2021. Following the update, a game I played regularly began to short-circuit, seemingly overnight. In some cases, teammates would wait for me for minutes on end to crawl through the loading screen, or it would fully crash. The screen could tear repeatedly, often accompanied by freezing. This issue wasn’t a fault in the update itself, but rather a new security feature that the game’s anti-cheat software wasn’t yet ready for.

Sure, I could’ve waited the short two weeks for the game to be patched. But I’m an impatient nerd who wanted the game to work right away, so I rolled back the previous night’s Windows Update, and voilà! All was well while playing the game once more, and after the game was patched two weeks later, I installed the update again, this time with no unfortunate follow-ups.

More commonly, smaller issues like this occur within Windows Updates when they fail to install, rather than with any new security feature that is introduced too soon. Issues from failed updates typically include features failing to start or work properly in some other way.

Windows updates can be a compatibility nightmare

Causing your rig and apps to not play nice, or not at all

The game I had trouble playing in 2021 was due to a compatibility issue arising between Windows 11 and the game’s anti-cheat. This is considered a relatively minor compatibility issue. Most compatibility issues, however, involve hardware, and they tend to take longer to resolve.

Another unfortunate common software update for various software was the introduction of the relatively recent TPM 2.0 requirement throughout the 2020s. Simply put, TPM is security tech built directly into the hardware of a motherboard. Certain apps have since required their users to enable TPM 2.0 on their motherboards, thereby abandoning support for prior TPM versions.

Some lucky users were able to access their BIOS and enable the feature by setting a specific option. Those on motherboards without TPM 2.0 functionality, however, were completely out of luck. The fix? If I want to use the app again, I’d need to buy a whole new motherboard. These compatibility issues are usually highlighted by a blatant reminder of what’s gone wrong in the form of an error message. Sometimes, especially in the case of a component’s driver raising a flag, it will instead cause any number of other symptoms related directly to whichever component’s driver is misbehaving.

These issues are also quite difficult to diagnose. Due to the numerous combinations of components, the OS, apps, games, and other software, one thing is certain: Finding others online who have experienced exactly the same issue can be harder than finding a needle in a haystack. Luckily, there isn’t much harm in just rolling back the last Windows Update anyway. If it hadn’t introduced the compatibility issue, I’d have been able to tell right away.

Updates can slow down or fully stop your PC in its tracks

Performance issues can be difficult to diagnose

Having your favorite app, game, or entire machine halt in its tracks over a broken or failed Windows Update is certainly frustrating, but also usually pretty obvious. On the other hand, an update may instead wreak havoc in a sneakier, much more difficult-to-notice way.

On the software side, this can take several different forms. Here are just a few issues that might pop up while using the program:

  1. The program takes a long time to open.
  2. The app’s features crash, hang, or don’t work properly.
  3. The program freezes or takes a very long time to perform tasks.
  4. It uses more of your system’s resources than usual (RAM or processing power are the usual culprits).

These issues can occur if a Windows Update installation fails after attempting to install a new feature or update that the application interacts with directly. In the same vein, if Windows itself is slowing down, throwing more blue screens as time passes, or Windows Explorer is freezing or crashing, it could be due to missing or corrupt registry or system files. In other words, the issue lies within the OS itself, rather than any specific application.

On the other hand, there are also behind-the-scenes hardware problems. These can range just as widely as potential software issues, as they can vary and behave differently based on your unique combination of components and apps installed. If a Windows Update of a component driver fails in this manner, symptoms related to that component may begin to appear unexpectedly.

For example, if a sound driver update fails for any reason and the installation of the update is corrupted, it may not be immediately apparent. At least, not until other symptoms begin to show, such as crackling audio, a crashing audio driver, or features like Windows Spatial Audio halting or behaving erratically.

Similarly, a failed RAM or other chipset driver installation can send your system’s resources out of whack. This can potentially starve certain programs of accessing the resources they need, such as CPU processing power or active memory. The list of possibilities here is long, but the solution is always the same: Roll back!

Both software and hardware issues in this category are typically caused by a failed update installation, too. But, just as in every category so far, there’s an impossibly long list of causes why this could happen, and trying to narrow down specifics may cost precious time.

If the OS or a PC component is involved, waiting could mean a later need to buy a replacement component or reinstall the OS.

Slow-burning, behind-the-scenes Windows Update issues can worsen over time when ignored.

Rolling back after all? Backup, backup, backup!

Don’t forget your safety goggles

If you’ve decided to pull the rollback trigger on a Windows Update, be sure to practice good PC hygiene. Back up your data and any important files, and take a Windows System Restore recovery snapshot. Close any programs. If you’re using the Windows Recovery Environment to find the update, you simply need to choose which update you want to get rid of, then go to town.

Once it’s begun, let the rollback finish completely before turning off the rig or attempting anything else with Windows Updates, such as rolling back another update, reinstalling, or checking for more updates. Good luck!

item.title: There has never been a better time to start self-hosting
item.itunes:title: There has never been a better time to start self-hosting
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
There has never been a better time to start self-hosting. We're storing more and more in the cloud, relying on third parties to keep our data safe and ensure it's always available. This works well for streaming services and similar platforms, but for sensitive information and other tasks, it's becoming more apparent that self-hosting is the way forward for many, myself included. Managing your own servers, software, and data is becoming more mainstream, having previously been viewed as niche and for enthusiasts only.

Today is as good a time as any to start self-hosting. Tools such as Proxmox make it easy to launch countless services and apps, and hardware is readily available, whether you wish to use standard consumer-grade parts or scour classified sites in the hope of finding some enticing deals on used enterprise gear. It's only set to continue to grow are more people look to decentralize themselves and take advantage of blockchain and peer-to-peer networks to achieve the same, but with fewer payments and more secure data vaults.

Self-hosting for the masses

From niche to mainstream

We've covered how network-attached storage (NAS) has become almost a staple in the modern home, creating a centralized hub for running services and storing data, but these compact servers have paved the way for self-hosting to take flight. Previously, you would need to know at least something about Apache, Linux, and some other technologies to host your own streaming service and automate the smart home, but it's now within reach of even those less tech-savvy.

One would naturally assume that the rise of cloud computing would diminish the appeal of self-hosting, but it created a hybrid model where the general public can leverage the scalability of the cloud for maximum availability without the technical know-how, while still being able to enjoy retaining control over much of their digital life. As more people become exposed to software such as Jellyfin and Home Assistant, self-hosting becomes a viable alternative to monthly subscriptions.

It's almost as easy as a single click to deploy services like Nextcloud, which can be used to effectively replace Google's own cloud-based productivity suite. There's so much documentation, too, which is especially handy for those looking to start their Docker journey. From building your own firewall with a mini PC or some custom system to deploying Proxmox and running your own virtual machines and Linux containers, you'll become a self-hosting master in no time.

I first started using NAS in 2015 and felt like I was late to the party, but I had no idea the segment would explode in popularity like it has. Synology is a renowned brand now, and other companies are making a name for themselves, too. We've got AMD launching HEDT processors that double up as excellent home lab platforms for running countless apps and services, as well as storing data. And we're storing a lot more data in 2025 than we did a decade ago. I've got recipes, photos, videos, backups, movies, shows, music, and much more.

More secure and affordable

Save your data and wallet

You've likely heard about all the data breaches companies have to recover from, and this is where data security comes into play. It's not that companies are inherently insecure because they often have far more robust security protocols than you or I, but they're targets of coordinated attacks. Black hat groups and individuals don't really care for the average Jane and Joe, but they will eye up Microsoft, Google, Apple, and all other companies to either directly attack their infrastructure or attempt to gain access to your accounts.

Should you utilize the NAS and bring everything in-house, you can often secure everything by simply locking all your hardware and data contained on devices behind a firewall and calling it a day. OPNsense by default fully protects your network from harm, and that's all there really is to it. It's only when you start to open up services and ports to the outside world that you will need to ensure checks are in place to prevent any vulnerabilities. But even then, we've got some excellent tools these days for doing just that.

Virtual private networks (VPNs) are a great choice for running secure tunnels between your home LAN and your device, should you be away. Reverse proxies are another option with countless possibilities for opening up specific parts of your LAN. Demilitarized zones (DMZs) and virtual LANs (VLANs) are other features of many firewalls and routers that can segment your network to prevent any compromised service or device from affecting the rest of the network. The best part? This is all basic network-level stuff that you can achieve in an hour or so.

You can even save some money, too. Remember Jellyfin and how it can catalog and stream all your purchased movies and TV shows? Now you can cancel Netflix and some other streaming services, using that saved monthly amount to fund the cost of more media to stream. Why this makes sense over having access to a wider online library is that should a time come that you end up cancelling these services for good, you're left with nothing. By purchasing all your media, you get to keep it for as long as your drives continue running.

Learn something new

Self-hosting is more accessible

As aforementioned, self-hosting and home labbing used to be hobbies reserved for those with the technical ability and know-how, but more people are opening up to the idea of heading into the command line interface. The self-hosting community is also thriving with a wealth of resources at the ready. Should you encounter problems, an expert is only a few clicks away, and we've got countless guides right here on XDA that can get you up and running in no time.

Through hosting your own content, you can troubleshoot issues, manage hardware and software deployments, and configure your network to achieve things not previously thought possible. Pick up some smart devices and link everything together to create a home that can not only respond to various inputs. For instance, I've configured my home office lighting to automatically turn on two hours before the sun sets, so long as either my power strip or my wife's pulls more than 0.5 amps of current.

Then, when it's ready to call it a day, the lights turn off if they're on, and both of our power strips pull less than 0.5 amps, to represent all computers and monitors cycling off. But Home Assistant will wait five minutes before disabling the smart bulbs to provide us time to leave the office, so it's not instant and requires us to locate the wall switch. It's the little things like this that can make a huge difference, and it's all possible through self-hosting your own apps. I just wish I had started this journey five years earlier.

It's not too late to take the leap

Self-hosting is more compelling than ever, and I've never seen the barriers to entry so low. Whether you’re motivated by a desire for autonomy, a passion for technology, or a commitment to ethical computing, now is the perfect time to begin. With the right tools, resources, and community support, your self-hosting journey can be both rewarding and transformative. Embrace the opportunity to take control of your digital life and discover the freedom that comes with owning your own technology.
item.description:
There has never been a better time to start self-hosting. We're storing more and more in the cloud, relying on third parties to keep our data safe and ensure it's always available. This works well for streaming services and similar platforms, but for sensitive information and other tasks, it's becoming more apparent that self-hosting is the way forward for many, myself included. Managing your own servers, software, and data is becoming more mainstream, having previously been viewed as niche and for enthusiasts only.

Today is as good a time as any to start self-hosting. Tools such as Proxmox make it easy to launch countless services and apps, and hardware is readily available, whether you wish to use standard consumer-grade parts or scour classified sites in the hope of finding some enticing deals on used enterprise gear. It's only set to continue to grow are more people look to decentralize themselves and take advantage of blockchain and peer-to-peer networks to achieve the same, but with fewer payments and more secure data vaults.

Self-hosting for the masses

From niche to mainstream

We've covered how network-attached storage (NAS) has become almost a staple in the modern home, creating a centralized hub for running services and storing data, but these compact servers have paved the way for self-hosting to take flight. Previously, you would need to know at least something about Apache, Linux, and some other technologies to host your own streaming service and automate the smart home, but it's now within reach of even those less tech-savvy.

One would naturally assume that the rise of cloud computing would diminish the appeal of self-hosting, but it created a hybrid model where the general public can leverage the scalability of the cloud for maximum availability without the technical know-how, while still being able to enjoy retaining control over much of their digital life. As more people become exposed to software such as Jellyfin and Home Assistant, self-hosting becomes a viable alternative to monthly subscriptions.

It's almost as easy as a single click to deploy services like Nextcloud, which can be used to effectively replace Google's own cloud-based productivity suite. There's so much documentation, too, which is especially handy for those looking to start their Docker journey. From building your own firewall with a mini PC or some custom system to deploying Proxmox and running your own virtual machines and Linux containers, you'll become a self-hosting master in no time.

I first started using NAS in 2015 and felt like I was late to the party, but I had no idea the segment would explode in popularity like it has. Synology is a renowned brand now, and other companies are making a name for themselves, too. We've got AMD launching HEDT processors that double up as excellent home lab platforms for running countless apps and services, as well as storing data. And we're storing a lot more data in 2025 than we did a decade ago. I've got recipes, photos, videos, backups, movies, shows, music, and much more.

You've likely heard about all the data breaches companies have to recover from, and this is where data security comes into play. It's not that companies are inherently insecure because they often have far more robust security protocols than you or I, but they're targets of coordinated attacks. Black hat groups and individuals don't really care for the average Jane and Joe, but they will eye up Microsoft, Google, Apple, and all other companies to either directly attack their infrastructure or attempt to gain access to your accounts.

Should you utilize the NAS and bring everything in-house, you can often secure everything by simply locking all your hardware and data contained on devices behind a firewall and calling it a day. OPNsense by default fully protects your network from harm, and that's all there really is to it. It's only when you start to open up services and ports to the outside world that you will need to ensure checks are in place to prevent any vulnerabilities. But even then, we've got some excellent tools these days for doing just that.

Virtual private networks (VPNs) are a great choice for running secure tunnels between your home LAN and your device, should you be away. Reverse proxies are another option with countless possibilities for opening up specific parts of your LAN. Demilitarized zones (DMZs) and virtual LANs (VLANs) are other features of many firewalls and routers that can segment your network to prevent any compromised service or device from affecting the rest of the network. The best part? This is all basic network-level stuff that you can achieve in an hour or so.

You can even save some money, too. Remember Jellyfin and how it can catalog and stream all your purchased movies and TV shows? Now you can cancel Netflix and some other streaming services, using that saved monthly amount to fund the cost of more media to stream. Why this makes sense over having access to a wider online library is that should a time come that you end up cancelling these services for good, you're left with nothing. By purchasing all your media, you get to keep it for as long as your drives continue running.

Learn something new

Self-hosting is more accessible

As aforementioned, self-hosting and home labbing used to be hobbies reserved for those with the technical ability and know-how, but more people are opening up to the idea of heading into the command line interface. The self-hosting community is also thriving with a wealth of resources at the ready. Should you encounter problems, an expert is only a few clicks away, and we've got countless guides right here on XDA that can get you up and running in no time.

Through hosting your own content, you can troubleshoot issues, manage hardware and software deployments, and configure your network to achieve things not previously thought possible. Pick up some smart devices and link everything together to create a home that can not only respond to various inputs. For instance, I've configured my home office lighting to automatically turn on two hours before the sun sets, so long as either my power strip or my wife's pulls more than 0.5 amps of current.

Then, when it's ready to call it a day, the lights turn off if they're on, and both of our power strips pull less than 0.5 amps, to represent all computers and monitors cycling off. But Home Assistant will wait five minutes before disabling the smart bulbs to provide us time to leave the office, so it's not instant and requires us to locate the wall switch. It's the little things like this that can make a huge difference, and it's all possible through self-hosting your own apps. I just wish I had started this journey five years earlier.

It's not too late to take the leap

Self-hosting is more compelling than ever, and I've never seen the barriers to entry so low. Whether you’re motivated by a desire for autonomy, a passion for technology, or a commitment to ethical computing, now is the perfect time to begin. With the right tools, resources, and community support, your self-hosting journey can be both rewarding and transformative. Embrace the opportunity to take control of your digital life and discover the freedom that comes with owning your own technology.

item.title: The 5 greatest music games people love to play together at parties
item.itunes:title: The 5 greatest music games people love to play together at parties
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
There was a time when music games were incredibly popular with everyone. The genre had a massive explosion in activity when Harmonix released their first Guitar Hero game, which became a big hit on multiple platforms. With a combination of classical and new age rock music, along with an interesting and new way to play, Guitar Hero inspired other developers to release music games that capitalized on the genre becoming a growing trend. Eventually, multiple music games were being played at parties and events, with full ensembles of instrument controllers to bring together people in makeshift bands, celebrating music and cooperative multiplayer gaming.

During that time, there were many options to choose from when it came to getting a music game on home consoles. Most people would stick with one series or another, but there was the occasional instance where someone would end up owning multiple games, with a full set up. These were great to bring out for home parties and large gatherings, since they always drew attention from everybody, especially if they loved the type of music being played. These are five of the greatest music games that people loved to play together at parties back then.

Guitar Hero II

A legendary track list

Credit: Harmonix

When everything in the music genre was blowing up in 2006, Guitar Hero II was the game that everyone pointed to as the big reason for it's fast-growing popularity. The first Guitar Hero game introduced people to the idea of rocking out with a controller shaped like a guitar, which was unique and very different at the time. But it was Guitar Hero II that took the concept further by refining the controls during gameplay, and putting together a track list of legendary rock music. It also took advantage of cooperative multiplayer for multiple songs, letting a pair of players work together to get the highest scores on tough tracks that required fast hand movement and careful timing when using up their meter. Multiplayer also had the Face-Off mode, which put two players against each other to see who had the best score, with a variation of the mode called Pro Face-Off.

Guitar Hero II had different releases on both the PlayStation 2 and the Xbox 360, with the latter version having a slightly altered list of songs. The games were fundamentally the same, but some tracks were exclusive to the Xbox 360 version, along with the ability to obtain new songs through downloadable content. The controller for the game was also different in its design, resembling the look of a Gibson X-Plorer guitar, rather than the original design from the first game that was reused for the sequel on PlayStation 2. The game was very popular and did well on both platforms, which opened up the door for more Guitar Hero games to be released, including band-specific spin-offs and sequels.

Guitar Hero II
Rhythm
Systems
Released
November 7, 2006
ESRB
T // Lyrics
Developer(s)
Harmonix Music Systems
Publisher(s)
RedOctane, Activision
Engine
game engine
3 Images

WHERE TO PLAY

PHYSICAL
Powered by

Rock Band

Getting the whole group together

In the wake of Guitar Hero's success, the next logical step for the genre was to try and put together an ensemble of instruments. Rather than just the guitar, things like the drums and vocals were something that helped make a complete band. This was the central selling point for Rock Band, which released in 2007. The game was released for Xbox 360, PlayStation 3, and the PlayStation 2, with all versions of the game being very similar. Rock Band was often packaged as a big bundle that included every instrument to play together, but later had bundles that included one or two instrument controllers instead to bring the cost down for customers.

With multi-instrument gameplay being the core part of the experience, the list of tracks included in the game became more diverse. Everything from hard rock to pop-rock and metal music was included in Rock Band, much to the delight of music lovers everywhere. The overall gameplay of Rock Band is very similar to what everyone sees in other music games, including the Guitar Hero and SingStar games. While the drums keep the beat, two guitar players play the lead guitar and bass parts of the music, while someone can sing lyrics on a microphone. It was the perfect set up for people at parties who love music and getting together with friends. The ability to download additional music off platforms like Xbox Live and PlayStation Network only made things better.

Rock Band
Rhythm
Music
Systems
Released
November 20, 2007
ESRB
T
Developer(s)
Harmonix
Publisher(s)
MTV Games
Multiplayer
Local Multiplayer
6 Images

WHERE TO PLAY

PHYSICAL
Powered by

Dance Dance Revolution Ultramix

Just dancing all the bad times away

Credit: Konami

One of the biggest music game franchises in the genre is Dance Dance Revolution. While first being a big hit in arcades, the series made the jump to home consoles across multiple platforms, including the PlayStation 2 and original Xbox. While the entry in the series on PlayStation 2 is arguably the most popular, the game on Xbox had extra features that many DDR fans loved. Dance Dance Revolution Ultramix is the first game of the series to release on Xbox consoles, and it also used more of the original visuals from the arcade version. Dance Dance Revolution Ultramix would also be the first to make some light changes with the way dancers move on screen during songs, such as moving to a fixed beat rather than the BPM of the song being played.

While the gameplay is the same across most DDR titles, the song lists between them are almost always different. Playing a DDR game often involves a dance pad connected to the console, where people can step onto the corresponding arrows as they appear on-screen. It's a popular series among people who love dance music, and while most prefer to play the game on the original arcade machines, it still had plenty of playtime among people at home. Dance Dance Revolution Ultramix would have additional tracks that could be downloaded from Xbox Live, with about 30 more songs added to the complete track list.

Dance Dance Revolution: Ultramix
Released
November 18, 2003
Powered by

Band Hero

Going beyond the guitar

As time went on, and the music genre began to die down in popularity, many of the series that were big in it needed to find ways to change. The explosive trend of music games was not like how it was back in the early 2000s, mostly due to the changes in economic times and the growing price tag for each new release in the genre. One of the last major releases for the Guitar Hero series was Band Hero. The game followed the same approach as Rock Band had done before, adding in more instruments for people to play together, with an entirely new group of music to explore. The game released in 2009 for multiple platforms, including versions for the Nintendo DS and Nintendo Wii, even though the popular versions of it were on the Xbox 360 and PlayStation 3.

Unlike previous years, this entry in the series didn't have as smooth of a release as other games. Between the shift in attitudes towards the music genre, and the controversy from the band No Doubt suing Activsion, it was an uneasy time. But that didn't stop Band Hero from having a few noteworthy points that fans of music games loved. Multiple pop artists lent their likeness to the game and parts of their music catalog for fans to play. One of the biggest additions to this was Taylor Swift, who appeared in the game as an avatar to sing some of her biggest hits at the time.

Credit: (YouTube) A Wise Moose

The gameplay of Band Hero on home consoles is exactly what you would expect, especially if you have played Rock Band before. While the guitar part was given some extra attention, the rest of the band is still a major part of the experience when playing through a song. The gameplay on the Nintendo DS version takes advantage of the dual-screen aspect of the system, while also having a Guitar Grip attachment for the DS to play the guitar sections. It was a very different way to play compared to other music games on portable systems, and it worked very well. However, the reception of Band Hero was mixed at the time for critics and fans, as the hype for the music genre continued to fall.

Band Hero
Action
Simulation
Music
Systems
Released
November 3, 2009
ESRB
E For Everyone 10+ // Lyrics, Mild Suggestive Themes
Number of Players
1-4
6 Images
Powered by

Just Dance

Great music for the perfect time

When it comes to getting people up and dancing to great music, there's no other game that does it better than Just Dance. Despite critics being harsh on the original game, it became a massive success worldwide for Ubisoft, which helped spawn multiple sequels for a number of platforms. Just Dance is a series that takes many of the popular and chart-topping tracks of the time and mashes them together with fantastic visuals and vibes for everyone to enjoy. It's arguably one of the best music games to bring out during gatherings, because the appeal is so wide with its large selection of music.

While the first game in the series had a great selection of musical artists featured in its set list, subsequent sequels took things further. Not only did the music selection get bigger, but various sequels also added in newer modes and tweaked aspects of the gameplay. Most of the dancing is done while holding a controller and moving to the rhythm and poses that are shown on screen, and players are scored based on their performance. Later games started to implement features from the camera that different consoles had available, as well as mixed up the gameplay with a variation to the dancing that took advantage of the pop themes that the series relished in. The most recent entry in the series, Just Dance 2026 Edition, includes a Co-Op party mode for multiple players to dance together.

Just Dance 2026 Edition
Party Game
Music
Systems
Released
October 14, 2025
ESRB
Everyone / Mild Cartoon Violence, Mild Lyrics, Comic Mischief
Developer(s)
Ubisoft
Publisher(s)
Ubisoft
Multiplayer
Local Multiplayer
7 Images

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL
Powered by

Music vibes throughout the years

Most people can connect and relate to one another through their appreciation of music. And when combined with video games, the results can lead to great things and fun times for everybody. While the music genre has gone through substantial changes over the years, we still have great games that blend our love for music and fun gameplay together in clever and interesting ways. We might not be rocking out with a guitar controller as often as we used to, but we all still love getting together and hearing some great tracks that get the party going.
